CFItems.fullStatement: Mental Health Professional
CFItems.notes:
**Prerequisites:** 
Introduction to Healthcare Science and Essentials of Healthcare.

CFItems.fullStatement: Demonstrate employability skills required by business and industry. *
CFItems.notes: The following standard is included in all CTAE courses adopted for the Career Cluster/Pathways. Teachers should incorporate the elements of this standard into lesson plans during the course. The topics listed for each element of the standard may be addressed in differentiated instruction matching the content of each course. These elements may also be addressed with specific lessons from a variety of resources. This content is not to be treated as a unit or separate body of knowledge but rather integrated into class activities as applications of the concept.

CFItems.fullStatement: Communicate effectively through writing, speaking, listening, reading, and interpersonal abilities.
Demonstrate creativity by asking challenging questions and applying innovative procedures and methods.
Exhibit critical thinking and problem solving skills to locate, analyze and apply information in career planning and employment situations.
Model work readiness traits required for success in the workplace including integrity, honesty, accountability, punctuality, time management, and respect for diversity.
Apply the appropriate skill sets to be productive in a changing, technological, diverse workplace to be able to work independently and apply team work skills.
Present a professional image through appearance, behavior and language.
Explore various careers that pertain to mental health services.

Identify and describe current employment options in mental and behavioral health professions, including required education, training, salary, and employment outlook for each option.
* Examples could include careers in Clinical Social Work, Counseling Psychology, Forensic Psychology, Mental Health Technician, Neurodiagnostic Technology, Nursing, Occupational Therapy, Psychiatry, Psychiatric Nurse Practitioner, Recreational Therapy, Registered Behavioral Technician, Sports Psychologists, Licensed Clinical Counselors, Specialty Counseling, etc.
Distinguish between the different levels of education, credentials, licensing, certifications, pay, and job outlook for careers in this field.
Identify other related learning opportunities for careers in this field, such as webinars, workshops, and conferences.
Describe the various roles and responsibilities in the scope of practice for a profession in mental and behavioral health services.
Explore professional disciplines and settings in mental health services, including:
Residential/Inpatient Facilities: regular and psychiatric hospitals, substance use recovery, rehabilitation centers, adolescent treatment facilities, geriatric facilities, etc.
Outpatient Facilities: Individual/family counseling, recovery houses, day treatment centers, support groups, alcohol and drug addiction centers, etc.
Telehealth services (mental health & psychiatry options)
School-based mental health programs (school counselors, social workers, mental health clinicians), evidence based best-practices, and research-informed practice.
Prevention services (ex: teen mental health first aid, suicide prevention, opioid prevention, etc.)
Identify and discuss the roles of the following government agencies that assist in this field:
The National Institute of Mental Health (NIMH)
The National Alliance on Mental Illness (NAMI); NAMI-GA
Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration (SAMHSA)
Mental Health America (MHA); MHA-GA
Mental Health.gov
The Department of Drug and Alcohol Programs (DDAP)
Georgia Department of Behavioral Health and Developmental Disabilities (DBHDD)
Local community agencies: Local mental health providers, community counseling and psychology services, nonprofit organizations, and advocacy organizations for mental health.
School-based health centers
Demonstrate an understanding of appropriate professional, ethical, and legal behavior.
Demonstrate techniques for maintaining confidentiality and patient privacy (protected health information) as outlined in HIPAA (Health Insurance portability and Accountability Act) regulations.
Recognize a personal code of ethics by demonstrating respectful and empathetic interactions and treatment of all clients (patients, consumers, residents, etc.)
Explain the Codes of Ethics for each mental health profession (ex: NASW Code of Ethics).
Compare and contrast various legal considerations such as advanced directives, living wills, DNR, etc.
Identify the power of attorney (POA) for clients with altered mental status and update documentation appropriately (including DNR (Do Not Resuscitate), DNI (Do Not Intubate), and/or code status).
Describe proper healthcare etiquette used in hospice environments, including the use of palliative treatment.
Discuss the client’s rights and protection of privacy, confidentiality, privileged communication, Informed Consent, Release of Information, and Mandated Reporter.
Describe the involuntary commitment and involuntary treatment process for suicidal or homicidal individuals’ client rights/legal documents.
Discuss the legality and use of forced treatment (hospitalization, medication, restraint, and seclusion) in crisis/emergency situations.
Maintain adequate documentation necessary to provide appropriate, confidential patient care.
Demonstrate the use of information management components, including electronic health records and documentation needed for authorized units from payor sources (public and private).
Demonstrate adhering to regulations regarding confidentiality and appropriate record release.
Create a client rotation schedule with client appointments that could be followed in a particular healthcare facility, school environment, telehealth setting, home health setting, etc.
Create an appropriate daily schedule of individual and group activities to be followed.
Identify potential educational needs of the client and the caregiver.
Develop instructions for family members to adhere to when caring for their loved one.
Explain the purpose of constantly interacting with and monitoring the behavior of each client.
Identify trends and changes in the mental and behavioral health industry.
Discuss the history, advancements, and emerging needs of mental and behavioral health.
Explain various support provided to individuals experiencing a mental health crisis in a school setting.

Describe changes currently occurring in the business and industry world in relation to employees and their mental and behavioral health (ex: safe rooms, stress relief rooms, aromatherapy, quiet space, therapy dogs, etc.)
Discuss various coping skills and life skills used in therapy groups.
Demonstrate stress reducing behaviors to initiate a positive climate for self and others.
Recognize and demonstrate an understanding of appropriate use of terminology within the field of mental and behavioral health.
Describe the difference between mental health and behavioral health.
Explain how behavior can reflect mental health struggles.
Compare and contrast mental health and mental illness.
Recognize and define medical terminology (including roots, prefixes, and suffixes) and abbreviations related to mental and behavioral health.
Define, identify symptoms, identify causes, and discuss a treatment plan for the following:
Anxiety Disorders (including panic disorders, obsessive-compulsive disorders, phobias, etc.)
Mood Disorders (including depression, bipolar disorders, etc.)
Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD)
Substance Use Disorders (including addiction).
Eating disorders
Explain the meaning of substance misuse, abuse, relapse, and detoxification.
Analyze possible origins of mental health disorders regarding the following causes:
Genetic
Brain chemistry
Social determinants in relation to social, emotional struggles
Trauma (the effects of trauma on the brain)
Socioeconomic disparities in the mental health systems
Discuss and provide examples of defense mechanisms (denial, distortion, repression, projection, etc.)
Compare mental health services within the home care setting versus the clinical setting.

Describe and differentiate between various mental health disorders related to children and teenagers (ages 4–16), such as anxiety disorders, attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), autism spectrum disorder (ASD), eating disorders, depression, post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD), and schizophrenia.

Describe and differentiate between various mental health disorders related to young adults and adults (ages 17–65), such as anxiety disorders, eating disorders, depression, post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD), and schizophrenia.

Describe and differentiate between various mental health disorders related to aging adults (ages 65 and older), such as PTSD, frontotemporal disorders, Alzheimer's, Parkinson’s, dementia, and cognitive decline.
List some warning signs and onset ages of mental illnesses that may occur in children, beginning at birth.
Describe signs and symptoms of early childhood mental health disorders from birth-5 years.
Explain the importance of early intervention as early as birth.
Analyze various early intervention methods and describe each rationale.
Explain the importance of forming healthy attachments between infants and young children with their caregivers through building caring relationships (the foundation of mental wellness).
Demonstrate an understanding of the importance of early social emotional development in the first years of life.
List some warning signs and onset ages of mental illnesses that may occur in adults.
Create a timeline of possible attributes that could be contributing factors to mental illness in a person’s life.
Analyze various cause and effect scenarios that are commonly discovered with adult onset of mental illness.
Compare and contrast between psychotherapy and medication treatment options (including antidepressants, anti-anxiety, mood-stabilizing, hypnosis, and antipsychotic medications).

Differentiate between the following modalities of therapy:
a. Behavioral Therapy 
b. Cognitive Behavioral Therapy 
c. Dialectical Behavioral Therapy 
d. Humanistic Therapy 
e. Integrative Therapy
Behavioral Therapy
Cognitive Behavioral Therapy
Dialectical Behavioral Therapy
Humanistic Therapy
Integrative Therapy
Explain the importance of attachment and the effects that are caused by the absence of attachment.
Demonstrate methods of delivering and obtaining information, while communicating effectively with patients, care givers, team members and management (in person and/or virtual environments).
Model appropriate verbal and nonverbal communication.
Identify common barriers to communication including physical, psychological, language, cultural, and socioeconomic differences.
Recognize appropriate communication skills to be applied when serving individuals who may have these communication barriers.
Demonstrate age-appropriate, positive communication skills between a client and a clinician in a variety of scenarios related to mental and behavioral health, including de-escalation methods.
Demonstrate proper elements of written and electronic/digital communication (spelling, grammar, formatting, tone, etc.).
Recognize appropriate delivery of oral and written communication with physicians, counselors, therapists, administrators, other healthcare professionals, clients, and families.
Research the practice of using a dyadic (family therapy model) approach when working with parents/care givers and children.
Demonstrate examples of using “person-centered” language techniques.
Explain how tone can affect the delivery of instruction to a client.
Demonstrate appropriate coping skills (interviewing techniques during assessments) that can assist in building a trusting relationship with both a client and client’s family.
Model an appropriate example of communicating with a child’s parent to assist with coping skills with a child that demonstrates mental health disorders.
Demonstrate appropriate communication and listening skills with co-workers and leadership to ensure important information is passed from oncoming and outgoing treatment teams during a shift change.
Identify proper safety protocol and protective procedures that should be followed working in mental services.
Describe various warning signs of potential safety concerns and threats to the clinician, coworkers, and/or client.
Discuss the purpose and procedures for reporting safety concerns and potential threats (whether virtual or in person) to the appropriate personnel.
Explain the purpose of properly completing an incident report in the case of an incident.
Successfully complete a non-violent crisis intervention training, including de-escalation, disengagement safety techniques, and physical intervention techniques.
Research effective positive reinforcement techniques to use when interacting with any age level.
Demonstrate appropriate clinician/practitioner self-awareness and self-care for personal health and wellness.

Research and explain how the following concepts can affect the level of self-care of a mental health provider: 
* Transference 
* Secondary trauma 
* Boundary setting 
* Avoiding burnout 
* Emotional self-awareness
Discuss common methods of financial support for mental services.
Identify different insurances that include mental health coverage and the documentation needed for authorized units from payor sources (public and private).
Discuss various ways that mental health services may be included in an employee’s benefits.
Identify services that may be included in a student’s individual education plan.
Discuss various free services through public and non-profit organizations.
Differentiate between Medicaid, private, and third-party payment options.
Discuss legislation that has evolved from providing mental health services such as the Mental Health Parity Act.
Identify services to help homeless individuals gain other resources like mental health treatment, housing, transportation, and employment.
Demonstrate CPR, First Aid and the AED utilizing current standards and opioid awareness.
Analyze differences in pulse and respiratory rates in adult, child, and infant.
Discuss the function of the respiratory and circulatory systems and what happens with these systems during cardiac arrest and then during cardiopulmonary resuscitation.
Demonstrate how to locate pulse points and how to check pulse and respiratory rates.
Explain steps in identifying signs of an opioid overdose and what to do when one is suspected.

Discuss support available in responding to a crisis (ex: 988, My GCAL 1-800-715-4225 and app, mobile crisis units, crisis intervention, suicide prevention, mental health first aid, certified peer specialists, etc.)
a. Explain the importance of suicide awareness. 
b. Discuss warning signs and behaviors that one may exhibit that may cause concern. 
c. List strategies for prevention of suicidal thoughts.
Explain the importance of suicide awareness.
Discuss warning signs and behaviors that one may exhibit that may cause concern.
List strategies for prevention of suicidal thoughts.

Successfully complete CPR, AED, and First Aid training (emphasizing performance skills in initial assessment, bleeding control, first aid for various shock victims, applying bandages/splints, and assessing/treating burns, poisoning, seizures, insect stings/bites, heat/cold exposure, and syncope) according to American Heart Association or American Red Cross, or other nationally recognized certifying agency.

Explore how related student organizations are integral parts of career and technology education courses through leadership development, school, and community service projects, entrepreneurship development, and competitive events.
Research the history of the state supported healthcare science CTSO (Career Technical Student Organization).
Discuss the mission, purpose, motto, colors, official dress, and other distinguishing characteristics of the state supported healthcare science CTSO.
Explain how participation in the state supported healthcare science CTSO can promote lifelong responsibility for community service and professional growth and development.

Create a personal leadership plan to participate in programs, conferences, community service and competitive events on the local, state, and national level that align with the competencies, skills, and knowledge of this course.
Demonstrate employability skills required by business and industry. *

Teachers should incorporate the elements of this standard into lesson plans during the course. The topics listed for each element of the standard may be addressed in differentiated instruction matching the content of each course. These elements may also be addressed with specific lessons from a variety of resources. This content is not to be treated as a unit or separate body of knowledge but rather integrated into class activities as applications of the concept.

CFItems.fullStatement: Communicate effectively through writing, speaking, listening, reading, and interpersonal abilities.
Demonstrate creativity by asking challenging questions and applying innovative procedures and methods.
Exhibit critical thinking and problem solving skills to locate, analyze and apply information in career planning and employment situations.
Model work readiness traits required for success in the workplace including integrity, honesty, accountability, punctuality, time management, and respect for diversity.
Apply the appropriate skill sets to be productive in a changing, technological, diverse workplace to be able to work independently and apply team work skills.
Present a professional image through appearance, behavior and language.
Design a mobile application and demonstrate utilization in a medical setting.
Design a mobile app using a simulated mobile app tool.
Demonstrate (in a simulated setting) training a healthcare provider how to teach a patient to utilize the mobile app.
Research devices used for remote patient monitoring.
Examine the information technology services in hospitals and the major functional categories.
Differentiate between hospital-based IT and provider offices and other medical facilities IT systems.
Explain the basic hospital IT infrastructure.
Evaluate a hospital IT organization and leadership and the clinical staff and hospital leadership (CEO, COO, CFO, and Chief Medical Informatics Officer (CMIO)).
Explain the variety of IT services in a hospital.
Describe the major functional categories of HIT in hospitals and the related applications including the administrative, financial, and clinical.
Demonstrate the three main groups of HIT applications in hospitals including the clinical functions of HIT in a simulated format.
Examine the administrative functions of HIT, including ADT (Admissions, Discharge, Transfer), and enterprise-wide scheduling.
Evaluate the financial functions of the HIT, including charge capture, revenue cycle, and patient accounting.

Research the clinical functions of HIT including:
* hospital departmental information systems
* hospital electronic prescription and pharmacy system, medication dispensation challenges, and robotic dispensation
* medical imaging and radiology systems
* laboratory information system
Investigate the concept and role of the clinical decision support systems in HIT.

Evaluate HIT applications for physicians and nurses at bedside, including the following:
* medical diagnosis
* Computerized Physician Order Entry (CPOE)
* computer-based patient record system
* nursing applications and documentation
* planning and outcome identification
* nursing care plan with effective documentation and charting.

Investigate medication administration, including the following:
* what can go wrong
* the right method of medication administration
* process automation
Research the functionality and role of the Electronic Health Record system.

Research the functionality and role of the Electronic Health Record (EHR) system, including major features and components, impact on patient safety and outcomes, and the benefits and implementation challenges.
Evaluate health information flow and differentiate open systems interoperability and closed isolated systems.
Describe what is meant by open systems and interoperability versus closed isolated systems.
Discuss the role of data repositories and the challenges with incoming data sources.
Describe health information flow in a fully integrated, open environment, as well as the role of EHR in this structure.
Explain how building an application in the cloud can promote interoperability through using APIs in the digital health setting.
Compare and contrast different cloud computing models and key providers (including AWS, Azure, and Google).
Explain cloud computing concepts such as configurable resources, infrastructure as a service, shared responsibility, and monitoring.
Define the key cloud provider and basic terminologies used within Health IT.
Discuss how a basic network diagram of resources in the cloud works and how to protect them with firewall technologies/logical access restrictions and other key security measures in the cloud.
Adhere to patient privacy, security, and confidentiality regulations.

Describe the concept and importance of patient privacy, security and confidentiality including the following:
* HIPPA security regulations
* protected health information and how to protect them
* security program model
* risk assessment and management
* risk identification and mitigation
Evaluate the challenges associated with implementation of the Health Information Exchange, CCDs (Continuity of Care Documents) and CCRs (Continuity of Care Record).
Research the concept and practice of Health Information Exchange (HIE), CCDs, and CCRs.
Explain the challenges in implementing the Health Information Exchange (HIE).
Describe how to overcome the connectivity issues between heterogeneous systems and data exchange challenges.
Describe the role of standards such as Health Level 7 (HL-7), Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine (DICOM), and solutions such as Service Oriented Architecture (SOA).
Assess the HIT project life cycle and the role of HIT project management.
Investigate the process and challenges of HIT system acquisition and implementation.
Explain the role and importance of project management.
Evaluate a HIT project life cycle.
Identify the HIT project management components and stages.
Explain project planning, work breakdown structure, and scheduling.
Describe the formal process of requirements identification, RFP preparation, assessment, evaluation, implementation strategies, and risk management.

Explore project management concepts including “waterfall development,” SCRUM development, Lean Development, and CI/CD (Continuous Integration/Continuous Delivery or Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment).
Explore the fundamental steps in a CI/CD software development lifecycle (including the role of DevOps, security testing, and the difference from traditional development methods.)
Evaluate the effectiveness and success of a successful telemedicine program.
Assess the telehealth industry and growth in Georgia and across the United States.
Evaluate nonclinical applications of telehealth.
Research the integrative strategies that bring these two modalities, telemedicine, and telehealth, together for a more connected care / patient medical home model(s).
Demonstrate (through simulation) appropriate video conferencing etiquette, proper record keeping, and presenter skills used in a telemedicine presentation.
Evaluate the necessary set-up of a telemedicine presenting/patient site and the medical provider site.
Perform (in a simulated lab setting) or observe clinical telemedicine applications, and proper storage and forwarding techniques of a telemedicine conference.
Demonstrate technical skills necessary for working with electronic health records.

Perform (simulated in a lab setting) or observe (in a health IT company or healthcare facility) skills related to utilization of hospital electronic health records and other health IT platforms including:
* eMAR
* patient charting
* reporting
* ePrescribing
* patient demographics
Evaluate the value of health analytics.
Analyze what clinical data can reveal about a patient’s health status.
Assess the use of health analytics and the potential for a move to personalized medicine.
Evaluate how technology can be used to enhance the overall healthcare experience.

Explore how related student organizations are integral parts of career and technology education courses through leadership development, school, and community service projects, entrepreneurship development, and competitive events.
Research the history of Future Business Leaders of America (FBLA) and/or Future Health Professionals (HOSA).
Discuss the mission, purpose, motto, colors, official dress, and other distinguishing characteristics of FBLA and/or HOSA.
Explain how participation in FBLA and/or HOSA can promote lifelong responsibility for community service and professional growth and development.

Create a personal leadership plan to participate in programs, conferences, community service and competitive events on the local, state, and national level that align with the competencies, skills, and knowledge of this course.
